name: Mock Test - 06
instruction: This question paper contains three sections – A, B and C. Each part is compulsory. Section - A has 20 MCQs, attempt any 16 out of 20. 3 . Section - B has 20 MCQs, attempt any 16 out of 20 Section - C has 10 MCQs, attempt any 8 out of 10. There is no negative marking. All questions carry equal marks.

questions.question_text: If the set Z of all integers, which of the following relation R is not an equivalence relation?
questions.question_html: If the set Z of all integers, which of the following relation R is not an equivalence relation?

options.text: x R y : if x {tex}\leq{/tex} y
x R y : if x = y
options.html: x R y : if x = y

options.text: x R y : if x {tex}\equiv{/tex} y |3|
x R y : if x – y is an even integer
options.html: x R y : if x – y is an even integer

questions.correct_option_text: x R y : if x {tex}\leq{/tex} y
questions.explanation: x R y : if x {tex}\leq{/tex} y 2 ,3 are in Z with {tex}2\leq3\ but\ 3\nleq2{/tex} Therefore,R is not symmetric,hence it is not an equivalnce relation.

questions.question_text: Find the value of f(0), so that the function {tex}f(x)=\frac{\sqrt{a^{2}-a x+x^{2}}-\sqrt{a^{2}+a x+x^{2}}}{\sqrt{a+x}-\sqrt{a-x}}{/tex} becomes continuous for all x, given by
questions.question_html: Find the value of f(0), so that the function {tex}f(x)=\frac{\sqrt{a^{2}-a x+x^{2}}-\sqrt{a^{2}+a x+x^{2}}}{\sqrt{a+x}-\sqrt{a-x}}{/tex} becomes continuous for all x, given by
questions.explanation: {tex}\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\sqrt{a^{2}-a x+x^{2}}-\sqrt{a^{2}+a x+x^{2}}}{\sqrt{a+x}-\sqrt{a-x}}{/tex} {tex}=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\sqrt{a^{2}-a x+x^{2}}-\sqrt{a^{2}+a x+x^{2}}}{\sqrt{a+x}-\sqrt{a-x}} \times \frac{\sqrt{a+x}+\sqrt{a-x}}{\sqrt{a+x}+\sqrt{a-x}}{/tex} {tex}=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\sqrt{a^{2}-a x+x^{2}}-\sqrt{a^{2}+a x+x^{2}}}{a+x-(a-x)}{/tex} {tex}x \frac{(\sqrt{a+x}+\sqrt{a-x}) \sqrt{a^{2}-a x+x^{2}}+\sqrt{a^{2}+a x+x^{2}}}{\sqrt{a^{2}-a x+x^{2}}+\sqrt{a^{2}+a x+x^{2}}}{/tex} {tex}=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{a^{2}-a x+x^{2}-\left(a^{2}+a x+x^{2}\right)}{2 x} \times \frac{\sqrt{a+x}+\sqrt{a}}{\sqrt{a^{2}-a x+x^{2}}+\sqrt{a^{2}}}{/tex} {tex}=\lim _\limits{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{-2 a x}{2 x} \times \frac{\sqrt{a+x}+\sqrt{a-x}}{\sqrt{a^{2}-a x+x^{2}}+\sqrt{a^{2}+a x+x^{2}}}{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{2 a \sqrt{a}}{a+a}{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{2 a \sqrt{a}}{2 a}{/tex} {tex}=-\sqrt{a}{/tex}

questions.question_text: If {tex}S=\left[\begin{array}{ll} a & b \\ c & d \end{array}\right]{/tex} , then adj A is
questions.question_html: If {tex}S=\left[\begin{array}{ll} a & b \\ c & d \end{array}\right]{/tex}, then adj A is
questions.explanation: {tex}S=\left[\begin{array}{ll} a & b \\ c & d \end{array}\right]{/tex} M 11 = d {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} A 11 = d M 12 =c {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} A 12 = -c M 21 =b {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} A 21 = -b M 22 =a {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} A 22 = a {tex}\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow &\operatorname{Ad}(A)=\left[\begin{array}{cc} d & -b \\ -c & a \end{array}\right] \end{aligned}{/tex}

questions.question_text: Which of the following is a convex set?
questions.question_html: Which of the following is a convex set?
questions.explanation: {tex}\left\{(x, y):x\geq 2,y\leq4\right\}{/tex} is the region between two parallel lines, so any line segment joining any two points in it lies in it. Hence, it is a convex set.

questions.question_text: The slope of the tangent to the curve x = a sint, y = a {tex}\left\{ {\cos t + \log (\tan \frac{t}{2})} \right\}{/tex} at the point ‘t’ is
questions.question_html: The slope of the tangent to the curve x = a sint, y = a {tex}\left\{ {\cos t + \log (\tan \frac{t}{2})} \right\}{/tex} at the point ‘t’ is
questions.explanation: Given, {tex} x=asint,y=a\left\{ { \cos t+\log (\tan \frac { t }{ 2 } ) } \right\}{/tex} {tex} { { dx } }{ { dt } } =a\cos t,\frac { { dy } }{ { dt } } =a\left[ -\sin t+\frac { 1 }{ \tan \frac { t }{ 2 } } .{ sec }^{ 2 }\frac { t }{ 2 } .\frac { 1 }{ 2 } \right] =a\left[ -\sin t+\frac { 1 }{ 2sin\frac { t }{ 2 } .cos\frac { t }{ 2 } } \right] =a\left[ -\sin t+\frac { 1 }{ sint } \right] =a\frac { { cos }^{ 2 }t }{ sint } {/tex} Slope of the tangent {tex}=\frac { { dy } }{ { dx } } =\frac { { \frac { { dy } }{ { dt } } } }{ { \frac { { dx } }{ { dt } } } } =\frac { { a\frac { { cos }^{ 2 }t }{ sint } } }{ { a\cos t } } =\cot t{/tex}

questions.question_text: For non-singular square matrices A and B of the same order, we have adj (AB) = ?
questions.question_html: For non-singular square matrices A and B of the same order, we have adj (AB) = ?
questions.explanation: We Know that (AB) -1 = {tex}\frac {adj (AB)}{|AB|}{/tex} adj (AB) = (AB) -1 .|AB| We also know that (AB) -1 = B -1 A -1 |AB| = |A|.|B| Putting them in (i) adj (AB) = B -1 A -1 .|A|.|B| adj (AB) = (B -1 ,|B|)(A -1 , |A|) adj (AB) = (adjB).(adjA)

questions.question_text: What is the nature of function f(x) = 7x-4 on R?
questions.question_html: What is the nature of function f(x) = 7x-4 on R?
questions.explanation: Strictly Increasing Let {tex}x 1{/tex} and {tex}x 2{/tex} be any two numbers in {tex}R{/tex} . Then {tex}x _1<x _2{/tex} . {tex} =>7 x_1-4<7 x_2-4 .{/tex} As {tex}f(x 1)<f(x 2){/tex} , thus the function {tex}f{/tex} is strictly increasing on {tex}R{/tex} .

questions.question_text: Minimise Z = – 3x + 4 y subject to x + 2y ≤ 8, 3x + 2y ≤ 12, x ≥ 0, y ≥ 0.
questions.question_html: Minimise Z = – 3x + 4 y subject to x + 2y ≤ 8, 3x + 2y ≤ 12, x ≥ 0, y ≥ 0.
questions.explanation: Objective function is Z = - 3x + 4 y ……………………(1). The given constraints are : x + 2y ≤ 8, 3x + 2y ≤ 12, x ≥ 0, y ≥ 0. The corner points obtained by constructing the line x+2y=8 and 3x +2y = 12 are (0,0),(0,2),(3,0) and (20/19,45/19) Corner points Z = 5x + 3 y O(0 , 0 ) 0 B ( 2 , 0 ) 10 C( 0 , 3 ) 9 D ( 20/19 , 45/19 ) 235/19 ……………….(Max.) Here , Z = -12 is minimum at C ( 4 , 0 )

questions.correct_option_text: It is not necessary that either A = 0 or, B = 0
questions.explanation: If the matrix AB is zero, then, it is not necessary that either A = 0 or, B = 0

questions.question_text: {tex}\mathop {Lt}\limits_{x \to 0} \;\;\;\frac{{x\cos x - \log (1 + x)}}{{{x^2}}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.question_html: {tex}\mathop {Lt}\limits_{x \to 0} \;\;\;\frac{{x\cos x - \log (1 + x)}}{{{x^2}}}{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation: {tex}\begin{gathered} \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{{x\cos x - \log (1 + x)}}{{{x^2}}}\end{gathered} {/tex} {tex} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{{\cos x - x\sin x - \frac{1}{{1 + x}}}}{{2x}} = \mathop {\lim }\limits_{x \to 0} \frac{{ - sinx - (x\cos x + \sin x) + \frac{1}{{{{(1 + x)}^2}}}}}{2}= \frac{1}{2}{/tex} ( using L’Hospital Rule)

questions.question_text: Maximize Z = – x + 2y, subject to the constraints: x ≥ 3, x + y ≥ 5, x + 2y ≥ 6, y ≥ 0.
questions.question_html: Maximize Z = – x + 2y, subject to the constraints: x ≥ 3, x + y ≥ 5, x + 2y ≥ 6, y ≥ 0.
questions.explanation: Objective function is Z = - x + 2 y ……………………(1). The given constraints are : x ≥ 3, x + y ≥ 5, x + 2y ≥ 6, y ≥ 0. Corner points Z = - x + 2y D(6,0 ) -6 A(4,1) -2 B(3,2) 1 Here, the open half plane has points in common with the feasible region. Therefore, Z has no maximum value.

questions.question_text: The coordinate of the point on the ellipse {tex}16 x^{2}+9 y^{2}=400{/tex} where the ordinate decreases at the same rate at which the abscissa increases, are
questions.question_html: The coordinate of the point on the ellipse {tex}16 x^{2}+9 y^{2}=400{/tex} where the ordinate decreases at the same rate at which the abscissa increases, are
questions.explanation: The ordinate decreases at the same rate at which the abscissa increases {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d t}=-\frac{d x}{d t}{/tex} given equation of ellipse is- {tex}16 x^{2}+9 y^{2}=400{/tex} {tex}32 \times \frac{d x}{d t}+18 y \frac{d y}{d t}=0{/tex} {tex}32 \times \frac{d x}{d t}=-18 y \frac{d y}{d t}{/tex} {tex}32 x=18 y{/tex} {tex}x=\frac{9 y}{16}{/tex} {tex}16 x^{2}+9 y^{2}=400{/tex} {tex}16 \times\left(\frac{9 y}{16}\right)^{2}+9 y^{2}=400{/tex} {tex}81 y^{2}+144 y^{2}=6400{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y=\frac{16}{3} \text { or } y=\frac{-16}{3}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x=3 \text { or } x=-3{/tex} Therefore, the required point is {tex}\left(3, \frac{16}{3}\right){/tex} .

questions.question_text: If {tex}f(x)=\frac{1}{1-x},{/tex} then the set of points of discontinuity of the function {tex}f(f(f(x))){/tex} is
questions.question_html: If {tex}f(x)=\frac{1}{1-x},{/tex} then the set of points of discontinuity of the function {tex}f(f(f(x))){/tex} is
questions.explanation: Given: {tex}f(x)=\frac{1}{1-x}{/tex} Clearly, {tex}f: R-\{1\} \rightarrow R{/tex} Now, {tex}f(f(x))=f\left(\frac{1}{1-x}\right)=\left(\frac{1}{1-\left(\frac{1}{1-x}\right)}\right)=\left(\frac{1-x}{-x}\right)=\left(\frac{x-1}{x}\right){/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} fof : {tex}R-\{0,1\} \rightarrow R{/tex} Now, {tex}f(f(f(x)))=f\left(\frac{x-1}{x}\right)=\left(\frac{1}{1-\left(\frac{x-1}{x}\right)}\right)=x{/tex} {tex}\therefore \text { fofof: }{/tex} {tex}R-\{0,1\} \rightarrow R{/tex} Thus, {tex}f(f(f(x))){/tex} is not defined at x = 0, 1 Hence, {tex}f(f(f(x))){/tex} is discontinuous at {0, 1}

questions.question_text: {tex}\frac{d}{{dx}}({\tan ^{ - 1}}(\cot x)){/tex} is equal to
questions.question_html: {tex}\frac{d}{{dx}}({\tan ^{ - 1}}(\cot x)){/tex}is equal to
questions.explanation: {tex}\frac{d}{{dx}}({\tan ^{ - 1}}(\cot x)){/tex} = {tex} \frac{d}{{dx}}({\tan ^{ - 1}}(\tan (\frac{\pi }{2} - x))) = \frac{d}{{dx}}(\frac{\pi }{2} - x) = - 1{/tex}

questions.explanation: R

questions.question_text: If e x + y = xy then {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}={/tex} ?
questions.question_html: If ex + y = xy then {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}={/tex} ?
questions.explanation: Given that xy = e x + y Taking log both sides, we get log e xy = x + y (Since log a b c = clog a b) Since log a bc = log a b + log a c, we get log e x + log e y = x + y Differentiating with respect to x, we obtain {tex}\frac{1}{x}+\frac{1}{y} \frac{d y}{d x}=1+\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} Or {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}\left(\frac{y-1}{y}\right)=\frac{1-x}{x}{/tex} Therefore, {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y(1-x)}{x(y-1)}{/tex}

questions.question_text: If tan –1 x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{10}{/tex} for some x {tex}\in{/tex} R, then the value of cot –1 x is
questions.question_html: If tan–1x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{10}{/tex} for some x {tex}\in{/tex} R, then the value of cot–1x is
questions.explanation: We know tan –1 x + cot –1 x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} . Thus, cot -1 x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}-\frac{\pi}{10}{/tex} (since {tex} {{\tan }^{ - 1}}x =\frac{\pi}{10}{/tex} ) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} cot -1 x = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}-\frac{\pi}{10}=\frac{2 \pi}{5}{/tex}

questions.question_text: If the polynomial equation a 0 x n + a n–1 x n–1 + a n–2 x n–2 + …. a 2 x 2 + a 1 x + a 0 = 0 n being a positive integer, has two different real roots α and β, then between α and β, the equation n a n x n–1 + (n–1) a n–1 x n–2 + … + a 1 = 0 has
questions.question_html:
If the polynomial equation a0xn + an–1xn–1 + an–2xn–2 + …. a2x2 + a1x + a0 = 0
n being a positive integer, has two different real roots α and β, then between α and β, the equation n anxn–1 + (n–1) an–1xn–2 + … + a1 = 0 has
questions.explanation: As the polynomial, na n x n–1 + (n–1) a n–1 x n–2 + … + a 1 = 0 is a derivative of the polynomial a 0 x n + a n – 1 x n – 1 + a n – 2 x n – 2 +..............a 2 x 2 + a 1 x + a 0 = 0 ...(i) Substituting x = 0 in equation (i), we obtain f(0) = a 0 < 0, {Y – Intercept of the graph is negative} On the other hand ...[ {tex}\because{/tex} an > 0 and ‘n’ is even, the leading term on x n , is positive for only x] For |x| to be large, we get the term a n x n will dominate, therefore {tex}\underset{x\rightarrow-\infty}{lim}{/tex} f (x) = {tex}+\infty{/tex} {tex}\underset{x\rightarrow\infty}{lim}{/tex} f (x) = {tex}+\infty{/tex} If {tex}\underset{x\rightarrow\infty}{lim}{/tex} f(x) = {tex}+\infty{/tex} , there must exist Same number {tex}\alpha{/tex} < 0, where f( {tex}\alpha{/tex} > 0) {tex}\therefore{/tex} f(0) = a 0 < 0, {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\alpha<\beta<0{/tex} , such that f( {tex}\beta{/tex} ) = 0 Also, there is some value 0 < {tex}\alpha{/tex} , Where f(a) & so there exists, 0 < b < a with f(b) = 0 Additionally, the polynomial function (equation (i)) is continuous everywhere in R and consequently derivative in R. {tex}\therefore{/tex} a 0 x n + a n – 1 x n – 1 + a n – 2 x n – 2 +..............a 2 x 2 + a 1 x + a 0 = 0 is continuous on {tex}\alpha, \beta{/tex} and derivative on {tex}\alpha, \beta{/tex} . Therefore,we see that it is satisfy the condition of Rolle' s Theorem. As per the Rolle’s Theorem, between any two roots of a function f(x), there exists at least one root of its derivative. Therefore, the equation na n x n–1 + (n–1) a n–1 x n–2 + … + a 1 = 0 will have at least one root between {tex}\alpha{/tex} & {tex}\beta{/tex} .

questions.question_text: If y = log 10 x then {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}={/tex} ?
questions.question_html: If y = log10 x then {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}={/tex} ?
questions.explanation: Here, y = log 10 x Using the property that log a b {tex}=\frac{\log _{e} b}{\log _{e} a}{/tex} , we obtain {tex}y=\frac{\log _{e} x}{\log _{e} 10}{/tex} Differentiating with respect to x, we {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{x \log _{e} 10}{/tex}

questions.question_text: Let {tex}f: R-\left\{\frac{3}{5}\right\} \rightarrow R{/tex} be defined by {tex}f(x)=\frac{3 x+2}{5 x-3}{/tex} . Then,
questions.question_html: Let {tex}f: R-\left\{\frac{3}{5}\right\} \rightarrow R{/tex} be defined by {tex}f(x)=\frac{3 x+2}{5 x-3}{/tex}. Then,
questions.explanation: Given function is {tex}f: R-\left\{\frac{3}{5}\right\} \rightarrow R{/tex} be defined by {tex}f(x)=\frac{3 x+2}{5 x-3}{/tex} fof(x) = f(f(x)) {tex}=f\left(\frac{3 x+2}{5 x-3}\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{3\left(\frac{3 x+2}{5 x-3}\right)+2}{5\left(\frac{3 x+2}{5 x-3}\right)-3}{/tex} on simplifying,we get, fof(x)= x => {tex}f^{-1}(x)=x{/tex}

questions.question_text: If {tex}y=\sqrt{x+\sqrt{x+\sqrt{x+}}} \ldots \infty{/tex} then {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}={/tex} ?
questions.question_html: If {tex}y=\sqrt{x+\sqrt{x+\sqrt{x+}}} \ldots \infty{/tex} then {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}={/tex} ?
questions.explanation: Given: {tex}\Rightarrow y=\sqrt{x+\sqrt{x+\sqrt{x+}}} \ldots \infty{/tex} We can write it as {tex}\Rightarrow y=\sqrt{x+y}{/tex} Squaring we get {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y 2 = x + y Differentiating with respect to x,we get {tex}\Rightarrow 2 y \frac{d y}{d x}=1+\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{(2 y-1)}{/tex}

questions.question_text: If the line y = x touches the curve y = x 2 + bx + c at a point (1, 1) then
questions.question_html: If the line y = x touches the curve y = x2 + bx + c at a point (1, 1) then
questions.explanation: y = x and y = x 2 + bx + c {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=1{/tex} and {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=2 x+b{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=1{/tex} and {tex}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{(1,1)}=2+b{/tex} Slope of the tangent = 1 2 + b = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} b = -1 Put, x = y = 1, b = -1 in y = x 2 + bx + c 1 = 1 - 1 + c {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} c = 1

questions.question_text: If f (x) = x tan -1 x then f ‘ (1 ) is equal to
questions.question_html: If f (x) = x tan-1 x then f ‘ (1 ) is equal to
questions.explanation: {tex}\begin{gathered} f'(x) = \frac{d}{{dx}}(x{\tan ^{ - 1}}x) = \frac{x}{{1 + {x^2}}} + {\tan ^{ - 1}}x\end{gathered} {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow f'(1) = \frac{1}{{1 + 1}} + {\tan ^{ - 1}}1 = \frac{1}{2} + \frac{\pi }{4}{/tex}

questions.question_text: sin -1 ( {tex}\frac{-1}{2}{/tex} ) + 2 cos -1 ( {tex}\frac{-\sqrt{3}}{2}{/tex} ) = ?
questions.question_html: sin-1({tex}\frac{-1}{2}{/tex}) + 2 cos-1({tex}\frac{-\sqrt{3}}{2}{/tex}) = ?
questions.explanation: Given: sin -1 {tex}\left ( \frac{-1}{2}\right){/tex} + 2 cos -1 {tex}\left ( \frac{-\sqrt{3}}{2} \right ){/tex} Let, x = sin -1 {tex}\left ( \frac{-1}{2}\right){/tex} + 2 cos -1 {tex}\left ( \frac{-\sqrt{3}}{2} \right ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x=-\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)+2\left[\pi-\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)\right]{/tex} ( {tex}\because{/tex} sin -1 (- {tex}\theta{/tex} ) = - sin( {tex}\theta{/tex} ) and cos -1 (- {tex}\theta{/tex} ) = - cos -1 ( {tex}\theta{/tex} )) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}-\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right)+2\left[\pi-\frac{\pi}{6}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}-\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right)+2\left[\frac{5 \pi}{6}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}-\frac{\pi}{6}+\frac{5 \pi}{3}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x=\frac{3 \pi}{2}{/tex}

questions.question_text: The equation of normal to the curve 3x 2 - y 2 = 8 which is parallel to the line x + 3y = 8 is
questions.question_html: The equation of normal to the curve 3x2 - y2 = 8 which is parallel to the line x + 3y = 8 is
questions.explanation: Given equation of the curve is 3x 2 - y 2 = 5 .... (i) Differentiating both sides w.r.t, we get {tex}6 x-2 y \frac{d y}{d x}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{3 x}{y}{/tex} , which is slope of tangent at any point on the curve {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} slope of normal at any point on the curve is {tex}-\frac{d x}{d y}=\frac{-y}{3 x}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad-\frac{y}{3 x}=-\frac{1}{3}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = x .... (ii) From (i) and (ii), we get 3x 2 - x 2 = 8 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 2 = 4 {tex}\Rightarrow \quad x=\pm 2{/tex} For x = 2, y = 2 [using (iii)] and for x = -2, y = -2 [using (iii)] Thus, the points on the curve at which normal to the curve are parallel to the line x + 3y are (2, 2) and (-2, -2). {tex}\therefore{/tex} Required equations of normal are {tex}y-2=-\frac{1}{3}(x-2){/tex} and y + 2 {tex}=-\frac{1}{3}(x+2){/tex} or 3y + x = 8 and 3y + x = -8

questions.question_text: The matrix {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 5 & 10 & 3 \\ -2 & -4 & 6 \\ -1 & -2 & b \end{array}\right]{/tex} is a singular matrix, if the value of b is
questions.question_html: The matrix {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 5 & 10 & 3 \\ -2 & -4 & 6 \\ -1 & -2 & b \end{array}\right]{/tex} is a singular matrix, if the value of b is
questions.explanation: {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 5 & 10 & 3 \\ -2 & -4 & 6 \\ -1 & -2 & b \end{array}\right]{/tex} is singular matrix. So its determinant value of this matrix is zero. i.e, {tex}\begin{vmatrix}5&10&3\\-2&-4&6\\-1&-2&b\end{vmatrix}\;=\;0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 5(-4b + 12) - 10(-2b + 6) + 3(4 - 4) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} -20b + 60 + 20b - 60 = 0 b does not exist

questions.question_text: The value of c in Rolle’s Theorem for the function f (x) = e x sinx, {tex}x \in[0, \pi]{/tex} is
questions.question_html: The value of c in Rolle’s Theorem for the function f (x) = ex sinx, {tex}x \in[0, \pi]{/tex} is
questions.explanation: Here, f(x) = e x sinx So, f '(x) = e x cosx + sinx e x Let c be the required point, then f '(c) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} e c (sin c + cos c) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin c = - cosc {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} tan c = -1 = tan (- {tex}\frac{\pi}4{/tex} ) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}c = n \pi- \frac{\pi}4{/tex} for n = 1, the principal value of c is: {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}c=\frac{3\pi}4{/tex}

questions.question_text: If {tex}\frac{d}{d x}{/tex} {x n - a 1 x n-1 + a 2 x n-2 + ... + (-1) n a n } e x = x n e x , then the value of {tex}a_{r}, 0<r \leq n,{/tex} is equal to
questions.question_html: If {tex}\frac{d}{d x}{/tex} {xn - a1 xn-1 + a2 xn-2 + ... + (-1)n an} ex = xn ex, then the value of {tex}a_{r}, 0<r \leq n,{/tex} is equal to
questions.explanation: {tex}\frac{d}{d x}\left\{x^{n}-a_{1} x^{n-1}+a_{2} x^{n-2}+\ldots .+(-1)^{n} a_{n}\right\} e^{x}=x^{n} e^{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}\left(\mathrm{nx}^{\mathrm{n}-1}-\mathrm{a}_{1}(\mathrm{n}-1) \mathrm{x}^{\mathrm{n}-2}+\mathrm{a}_{2}(\mathrm{n}-2) \mathrm{x}^{\mathrm{n}-3}+\ldots+(-1)^{\mathrm{p}-1} \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{n}-1}+\mathrm{x}^{\mathrm{n}}-\mathrm{a}_{1} \mathrm{x}^{\mathrm{n}-1}+\mathrm{a}_{2} \mathrm{x}^{\mathrm{n}-2}+\ldots+(-1)^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{n}}\right)=\mathrm{x}{/tex} {tex}e^x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}\left(\mathrm{x}^{\mathrm{n}}+\left(\mathrm{n}-\mathrm{a}_{1}\right) \mathrm{x}^{\mathrm{n}-1}-\left(\mathrm{a}_{1}(\mathrm{n}-1)-\mathrm{a}_{2}\right) \mathrm{x}^{\mathrm{n}-2}+\left(\mathrm{a}_{2}(\mathrm{n}-2)-\mathrm{a}_{3}\right) \mathrm{x}^{\mathrm{n}-3}-\ldots\right)=\mathrm{x}^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}{/tex} On comparing both sides we get n - a 1 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a 1 = n a 1 (n - 1) - a 2 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a 2 = a 1 (n - 1) = n (n - 1) a 2 (n - 2) - a 3 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a 3 = a 2 (n - 2) = n (n - 1) (n - 2) So, a r = n (n - 1) (n - 2) ... (n - (r - 1) ) = {tex}\frac{n !}{(n-r) !}{/tex}

questions.question_text: The maximum value of {tex}\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)^{x}{/tex} is:
questions.question_html: The maximum value of {tex}\left(\frac{1}{x}\right)^{x}{/tex} is:
questions.explanation: Let y = f(x) = {tex}\frac{1}{x}^x{/tex} Then, log y = log {tex}\frac{1}{x}^x{/tex} = xlog {tex}\frac{1}{x}{/tex} = -xlogx {tex} \Rightarrow \frac { 1 }{ y } \frac { { dy } }{ { dx } } =-\left( x.\frac { 1 }{ x } +logx.1 \right) =-\left( 1+\log x \right){/tex} {tex} \\ \Rightarrow f'(x)=-{ { \frac { 1 }{ x } }^{ x } }\left( 1+\log x \right) \quad {/tex} {tex}f'(x)=0\Rightarrow (1+\log x)=0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \log x=-1\Rightarrow x=\frac { 1 }{ e } {/tex} The maximum value of f(x) = {tex}= f(\frac{1}{e})=e^{ 1/e }{/tex}

questions.question_text: The principal value of tan -1 {tex}(-\sqrt3){/tex} is
questions.question_html: The principal value of tan-1 {tex}(-\sqrt3){/tex} is
questions.explanation: Let the principle value be given by x also, let x = tan -1 {tex}(-\sqrt3){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} tan x = {tex}-\sqrt{3}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} tan x = -tan {tex}\left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right)\left(\because \tan \left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right)=-\sqrt{3}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} tan x = tan {tex}\left(-\frac{\pi}{3}\right)(\because-\tan (\theta)=\tan (-\theta)){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}-\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex}

questions.question_text: For any two matrices A and B,
questions.question_html: For any two matrices A and B,

options.text: Whenever AB exists, then BA exists
options.html: Whenever AB exists, then BA exists

options.text: Sometimes AB = BA and sometimes AB {tex}\ne{/tex} BA
options.html: Sometimes AB = BA and sometimes AB {tex}\ne{/tex} BA

questions.correct_option_text: Sometimes AB = BA and sometimes AB {tex}\ne{/tex} BA
questions.explanation: If the two matrices A and B are of same order it is not necessary that in every situation AB = BA AB = BA = I only when A = B -1 B = A -1 Other time AB {tex}\ne{/tex} BA

questions.question_text: {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ll} \log _{3} 512 & \log _{4} 3 \\ \log _{3} 8 & \log _{4} 9 \end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ll} \log _{2} 3 & \log _{8} 3 \\ \log _{3} 4 & \log _{3} 4 \end{array}\right|{/tex} =
questions.question_html: {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ll} \log _{3} 512 & \log _{4} 3 \\ \log _{3} 8 & \log _{4} 9 \end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ll} \log _{2} 3 & \log _{8} 3 \\ \log _{3} 4 & \log _{3} 4 \end{array}\right|{/tex} =
questions.explanation: {tex}\left|\begin{array}{cc} \log _{3} 512 & \log _{4} 3 \\ \log _{3} 8 & \log _{4} 9 \end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ll} \log _{2} 3 & \log _{8} 3 \\ \log _{3} 4 & \log _{3} 4 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\left|\begin{array}{cc} 9 \log _{3} 2 & \log _{4} 3 \\ 3 \log _{3} 2 & 2 \log _{4} 3 \end{array}\right|{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ll} \log _{2} 3 & \frac{1}{3} \log _{2} 3 \\ 2 \log _{3} 2 & 2 \log _{3} 2 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = log 3 2 log 4 3 log 2 3 log 3 2 {tex}\left|\begin{array}{ll} 9 & 1 \\ 3 & 2 \end{array}\right| \times\left|\begin{array}{ll} 1 & \frac{1}{3} \\ 2 & 2 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left|\begin{array}{ll} 9 & 1 \\ 3 & 2 \end{array}\right| \times\left|\begin{array}{ll} 1 & \frac{1}{3} \\ 2 & 2 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left|\begin{array}{cc} 9+2 & 3+2 \\ 3+4 & 1+4 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left|\begin{array}{ll} 11 & 5 \\ 7 & 5 \end{array}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}(55-35){/tex} = 10

questions.question_text: Let x, y be two variables and x > 0, xy = 1 then minimum value of x + y is
questions.question_html: Let x, y  be two variables and x > 0, xy = 1 then minimum value of x + y is
questions.explanation: Given xy = 1. To find minimum value of x + y {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {tex}\frac{1}{x}{/tex} f(x) = x + {tex}\frac{1}{x}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f'(x) = 1 - {tex}\frac{1}{x^{2}}{/tex} To find local maxima or minima we have f'(x) = 0 1 - {tex}\frac{1}{x^{2}}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\pm{/tex} 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {tex}\pm{/tex} 1 But given that x > 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 1, y = 1 f"(x) = {tex}\frac{2}{x^{3}}{/tex} f"(1)= 2 > 0 function has minima at x = 1 f(1) = 2.

questions.question_text: The function f(x) = e –|x| is
questions.question_html: The function f(x) = e–|x| is

options.text: continuous everywhere but not differentiable at x = 0
options.html: continuous everywhere but not differentiable at x = 0

questions.correct_option_text: continuous everywhere but not differentiable at x = 0
questions.explanation: Given that f(x) = e –|x| {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=\left\{\begin{array}{l} \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{x}}, \mathrm{x}<0 \\ 1, \mathrm{x}=0 \\ \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{x}}, \mathrm{x}>0 \end{array}\right\}{/tex} Checking continuity and differentiability at x = 0, LHL: {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{-}} f(x)=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} f(0-h)=\operatorname{lime} \limits_{h \rightarrow 0}^{-h}=1{/tex} RHL: {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} f(x)=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} f(0+h)=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} e^{-h}=1{/tex} And f(0)= 1 {tex}\therefore{/tex} LHL = RHL = f(0) f(x) is continuous at x = 0. LHD at x =0, {tex}\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 0^{-}} \frac{f(x)-f(0)}{x-0}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(0-h)-f(0)}{0-h-0}{/tex} {tex}=\lim \limits_{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{e^{-h}-(0)}{-h}=\infty{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} LHD does not exist, so f(x) is not differentiable at x = 0

questions.question_text: Let f : R - {n} {tex}\to{/tex} R be a function defined by {tex}f(x)=\frac{x-m}{x-n}{/tex} , where m {tex}\ne{/tex} n. Then,
questions.question_html: Let f : R - {n} {tex}\to{/tex} R be a function defined by {tex}f(x)=\frac{x-m}{x-n}{/tex}, where m {tex}\ne{/tex} n. Then,
questions.explanation: Given that f : R - {n} {tex}\to{/tex} R where {tex}f(x)=\frac{x-m}{x-n}{/tex} , such that m {tex}\ne{/tex} n Let f(x) = f(y) {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{x-m}{x-n}=\frac{y-m}{y-n}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - m)(y - n) = (x - n)(y - m) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} xy - xn - my + mn = xy - xm - ny + mn {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = y So, f is one-one. {tex}f(x)=\frac{x-m}{x-n}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=\frac{\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{n}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y(x - n) = (x - m) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} xy - ny = x - m {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x(y -1) = ny - m {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=\frac{\mathrm{ny}-\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{y}-1}, \mathrm{y} \neq 1{/tex} For y = 1 , no x is defined. So, f is into.

questions.question_html: (tan-12 + tan-13) = ?
questions.explanation: Given: tan -1 2 + tan -1 3 Since we know that tan -1 x + tan -1 y = tan -1 {tex}\left(\frac{x+y}{1-x y}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} tan -1 2 + tan -1 3 = tan -1 {tex}\left(\frac{2+3}{1-(2 \times 3)}\right){/tex} {tex}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{5}{-5}\right){/tex} = tan -1 (-1) since the principle value of tan lies in the range [0, {tex}\pi{/tex} ] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} tan -1 (-1) = {tex}\frac{3 \pi}{4}{/tex}

questions.question_text: The void relation (a subset of A {tex}\times{/tex} A ) on a nonempty set A is:
questions.question_html: The void relation (a subset of A {tex}\times{/tex} A ) on a nonempty set A is:
questions.explanation: The relation { } {tex} \subset {/tex} A {tex}\times{/tex} A on A is surely not reflexive. However, neither symmetry nor transitivity is contradicted. So { } is a transitive and symmetric relation on A.

questions.question_text: Instructions for Question 46 to 50: A manufacture produces three stationery products Pencil, Eraser and Sharpener which he sells in two markets. Annual sales are indicated below: Market Products (in numbers) Pencil Eraser Sharpener A 10,000 2,000 18,000 B 6,000 20,000 8,000 If the unit Sale price of Pencil, Eraser and Sharpener are ₹2.50, ₹1.50 and ₹1.00 respectively, and unit cost of the above three commodities are ₹2.00, ₹1.00 and ₹0.50 respectively, then, Based on the information given above, answer the following questions: Total revenue of market A: ₹64,000 ₹60,400 ₹46,000 ₹40600
